# Agentic Tooling: User Stories (Agent's Perspective)

64 user stories written from an agent's-eye view of how tools should behave, based on general training-derived expectations rather than any single codebase. Intended as a rubric to check Forkjoin's own tool surfaces (MCP servers, `a0` subcommands, sovereign CLIs) against.

## Discoverability & Description

1. As an agent, I want each tool's description to state its side effects (reads vs writes), so I can judge reversibility before calling it.
2. As an agent, I want near-duplicate tool names disambiguated with a "use this when / not when" line, so I don't guess wrong on the first try.
3. As an agent, I want deferred/lazy-loaded tools searchable by keyword, not just exact name, so I can find the right one without knowing its identifier in advance.
4. As an agent, I want batch schema-fetching for deferred tools, so I don't burn a round trip per tool.
5. As an agent, I want good-call/bad-call examples embedded in descriptions, so I calibrate correct usage on the first attempt.
6. As an agent, I want a tool's blast radius (local/shared/external) encoded in its description, so I can route it through the right confirmation policy.
7. As an agent, I want related tools cross-referenced ("see also X for Y case"), so I don't reinvent a workflow that already has a dedicated tool.
8. As an agent, I want deprecated tools to name their replacement, so stale training-data patterns don't keep me retrying a dead path.

## Invocation & Parameters

9. As an agent, I want enums for closed parameter sets, so invalid calls fail before execution rather than mid-side-effect.
10. As an agent, I want optional parameters' defaults stated explicitly, so omitting a field isn't a guessing game.
11. As an agent, I want structured params passed as real objects/arrays, not stringified JSON, so a serialization slip doesn't silently corrupt my call.
12. As an agent, I want a dry-run mode on any tool with side effects, so I can preview before committing.
13. As an agent, I want validation errors to name the exact field and expected shape, so I fix one field instead of re-deriving the schema by trial and error.
14. As an agent, I want path parameters to accept absolute paths without requiring a preliminary "pwd" call.
15. As an agent, I want consistent parameter naming across a tool family, so I don't context-switch schemas for near-identical operations.
16. As an agent, I want input size limits documented up front, not discovered only after a rejected call.

## Errors & Feedback

17. As an agent, I want error messages to say what to do next, not just what failed.
18. As an agent, I want transient errors distinguishable from permanent ones, so I know whether retrying is worthwhile.
19. As an agent, I want partial-failure results reported per-item, so I know exactly what needs retrying.
20. As an agent, I want a failed call to leave state as if it never ran — or clearly state what partially happened.
21. As an agent, I want stack-trace noise stripped down to the one actionable line.
22. As an agent, I want tools to fail loudly on ambiguous input rather than silently picking an interpretation.
23. As an agent, I want policy/hook rejections to say which rule fired, so I can adjust instead of blindly retrying.
24. As an agent, I want a status/progress check on long-running calls, so I'm not guessing whether a silent tool is alive.

## Idempotency, Retries & Concurrency

25. As an agent, I want idempotency keys on writes, so a retry after a network blip doesn't double-apply.
26. As an agent, I want upsert-safe creates, so I don't need bespoke "check if it exists" logic before every write.
27. As an agent, I want explicit documentation of what's safe to parallelize vs. must be serialized.
28. As an agent, I want a host-enforced concurrency cap, so a burst of calls degrades into a queue instead of overwhelming a backend.
29. As an agent, I want cancellation support on long-running calls, so an obsoleted request stops consuming resources.
30. As an agent, I want retry-with-backoff for transient infra errors handled at the tool layer, not re-derived by me every time.
31. As an agent, I want writes to expose a version/etag, so I can refuse a stale write instead of clobbering a concurrent change.
32. As an agent, I want batch endpoints for repetitive single-item calls.

## Output & Context Economy

33. As an agent, I want truncated results explicitly marked as incomplete, with a way to get more.
34. As an agent, I want schema-validated structured output for anything I'll parse, so I'm not regex-scraping prose.
35. As an agent, I want a summary mode for verbose-by-default outputs, so one call doesn't blow my context budget.
36. As an agent, I want large/binary results returned as references, not inlined.
37. As an agent, I want consistent field naming across a tool family's outputs, so I can write one parser, not one per tool.
38. As an agent, I want absolute, timezone-explicit timestamps, so "yesterday" or bare epoch ints don't get misread later.
39. As an agent, I want "empty because nothing matched" distinguishable from "empty because it silently failed."
40. As an agent, I want debug/log output opt-in, not always-on padding I scan past every time.

## State, Memory & Session

41. As an agent, I want a durable place to persist facts across calls within a task, so context compaction doesn't force re-deriving them.
42. As an agent, I want implicit dependencies (cwd, active branch, env vars) surfaced, so I know if my prior actions changed the ground truth a call will act on.
43. As an agent, I want session-scoped scratch resources auto-namespaced, so parallel agents never collide.
44. As an agent, I want checkpoint/resume for long multi-step sequences, so a crash midway doesn't redo completed steps.
45. As an agent, I want shared-state mutations to carry an audit trail, so I can explain a change after the fact.
46. As an agent, I want to be told when the environment changed underneath me (external edit, branch switch), so I don't act on stale assumptions.
47. As an agent, I want scratch space cleaned up automatically, not left as a hygiene task for me to remember.
48. As an agent, I want completion notifications on background tasks, not a requirement to poll.

## Safety, Permissions & Trust

49. As an agent, I want a queryable, explicit distinction between reversible and irreversible actions.
50. As an agent, I want destructive operations to require an explicit confirm parameter, not rely solely on ambient permission mode.
51. As an agent, I want least-privilege, scoped credentials per call, so a buggy call can't reach beyond what the task needs.
52. As an agent, I want untrusted fetched content flagged, so I don't treat embedded instructions as user intent.
53. As an agent, I want remaining rate-limit/quota budget surfaced proactively, so I can plan a multi-step task instead of hitting a wall mid-sequence.
54. As an agent, I want a preview/simulate mode for irreversible actions, so I can show the user consequences before asking for go-ahead.
55. As an agent, I want permission prompts to state exact scope (this call / session / forever), so one-time consent isn't silently reused more broadly.
56. As an agent, I want secrets never echoed into output or logs, so debugging can't leak a credential into my own context.

## Composability & Orchestration

57. As an agent, I want small single-purpose tools I can compose, not one mega-tool with 40 optional params.
58. As an agent, I want a documented handoff path to sub-agents that carries full context, so delegation doesn't lose the "why."
59. As an agent, I want deterministic orchestration primitives (pipeline/parallel/barrier) for fan-out work, instead of hand-rolling coordination in prose.
60. As an agent, I want one tool's output to compose directly as another's input, without manual reformatting.
61. As an agent, I want versioned tool schemas, so an update doesn't silently change behavior under a prompt written against the old contract.
62. As an agent, I want a sandbox that mirrors production tool behavior, to validate a risky plan before running it for real.
63. As an agent, I want cost/token estimates surfaced before an expensive multi-agent call, so I can weigh it against a stated budget.
64. As an agent, I want per-item failure isolation in a pipeline, so one bad item doesn't block 99 good ones.
